course_info.title: Digital Forensics
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week
prerequisites: CY201 - Ethical Hacking and Penetration Testing

learning_objectives: Describe the digital forensics investigation process
Understand chain of custody and legal admissibility requirements
Apply forensic principles to preserve evidence integrity
readings: Guide to Computer Forensics and Investigations Ch. 1-2
NIST SP 800-86 Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital forensics is the science of identifying, preserving, analyzing, and presenting digital evidence in a manner that is legally admissible. Every action must be documented and defensible.
key_concepts: Forensic process: Identification, Preservation, Analysis, Presentation
Chain of custody documentation
Write-blocking and evidence integrity
Legal considerations: search warrants, consent, privacy laws
Forensic imaging: bit-for-bit vs logical acquisition
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a forensic image of a USB drive using dd and dc3dd, verifying integrity with SHA-256 hashes

review.value: Documented chronological record of who handled evidence, when, what they did, and where it was stored. Any break in the chain can render evidence inadmissible.
Hardware or software mechanism that prevents any modification to the evidence drive during acquisition. Essential for maintaining evidence integrity.

class_schedule.topic: Disk Forensics and File System Analysis
learning_objectives: Analyze NTFS and ext4 file system structures forensically
Recover deleted files using file carving techniques
Interpret Master File Table entries and inode structures

lecture_notes.introduction: File systems contain a wealth of forensic evidence beyond the files themselves. Understanding their internal structures enables recovery of deleted data and timeline reconstruction.
key_concepts: NTFS: MFT entries, $STANDARD_INFORMATION vs $FILE_NAME timestamps
ext4: inodes, journal, extent trees
Deleted file recovery: MFT entry reuse, inode unlink
File carving: header/footer signature scanning
Slack space and unallocated space analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using Autopsy and The Sleuth Kit to recover deleted files from an NTFS image, analyzing MFT entries to reconstruct the deletion timeline
setup_requirements: Sample forensic images with deleted files

review.value: MFT stores two sets of timestamps: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (modifiable by user) and $FILE_NAME (only modified by the kernel). Discrepancies indicate timestomping.
Scanning raw disk sectors for file headers (e.g., JPEG: FF D8 FF, PDF: %PDF) to recover files regardless of file system metadata status

learning_objectives: Acquire volatile memory from running systems
Analyze memory dumps with Volatility framework
Extract processes, network connections, and injected code from memory
readings: The Art of Memory Forensics Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: RAM contains evidence that exists nowhere else: running processes, network connections, encryption keys, and malware that never touches disk. Memory forensics is essential for incident response.
key_concepts: Memory acquisition: FTK Imager, WinPMEM, LiME
Volatility framework: profiles, plugins, analysis workflow
Process analysis: pslist, pstree, malfind
Network artifact extraction: netscan, connections
Code injection detection: hollow process, DLL injection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Acquiring memory from a compromised Windows VM, using Volatility to identify a hidden malware process and extract its C2 server address
setup_requirements: Windows VM with simulated malware infection

review.value: pslist walks the active process list (EPROCESS linked list). psscan scans all memory for EPROCESS structures, finding hidden/unlinked processes.
Identifies injected code by finding memory regions with executable permissions that were not loaded from a file on disk. Primary tool for detecting code injection.

learning_objectives: Reconstruct network events from packet captures
Correlate log entries across multiple systems
Build forensic timelines from network evidence
readings: Network Forensics: Tracking Hackers through Cyberspace Ch. 3-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Network forensics examines the traces attackers leave in packet captures, flow records, and logs as they move through a network.
key_concepts: Packet capture analysis: TCP stream reconstruction
HTTP session reconstruction and file extraction
DNS query log analysis for threat hunting
Log correlation across firewall, IDS, and server logs
Timeline construction: super-timeline methodology
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reconstructing an entire intrusion from a pcap file: identifying the initial exploit, lateral movement, and data exfiltration

review.value: Reassembling TCP streams from individual packets to reconstruct complete HTTP sessions, file transfers, and command sessions
Combining timestamps from all evidence sources (file system, registry, logs, memory) into a single chronological view of all system activity

learning_objectives: Extract forensic artifacts from the Windows registry
Analyze Windows event logs for evidence of compromise
Recover browser history, Prefetch, and SRUM data

lecture_notes.introduction: Windows systems contain hundreds of forensic artifacts across the registry, event logs, and system databases that tell the story of what happened on the machine.
key_concepts: Registry forensics: SAM, SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, NTUSER.DAT hives
UserAssist, ShimCache, Amcache for program execution evidence
Prefetch files: evidence of program execution with timestamps
Windows Event Logs: Security (4624, 4625, 4672), Sysmon
SRUM database: network usage, application resource consumption
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a forensic image with RegRipper to extract user activity, correlating with event logs to build an evidence timeline

review.value: Application Compatibility Cache records executable path and last modification time for programs that were executed or present on the system. Persists across reboots.
Successful logon event. Logon Type 2 (interactive), Type 3 (network), Type 10 (RDP). Combined with 4672 (special privileges assigned), reveals lateral movement.

learning_objectives: Describe mobile device forensic acquisition methods
Analyze iOS and Android forensic artifacts
Understand cloud forensics challenges and techniques

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile devices and cloud services are increasingly the primary evidence sources in investigations. Their unique architectures present distinct forensic challenges.
key_concepts: Mobile acquisition types: manual, logical, file system, physical
iOS forensic artifacts: keychain, SQLite databases, plists
Android forensic artifacts: SQLite databases, shared preferences, APK analysis
Cloud forensics: preservation requests, API-based collection
Legal challenges: encryption, jurisdiction, cross-border data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performing a logical acquisition of an Android test device and analyzing the SQLite databases for messaging and location artifacts

review.value: iOS devices use hardware-backed encryption with a unique per-device key. Full physical acquisition requires either the passcode or exploitation of a bootrom vulnerability (e.g., checkm8).
Data may exist only in cloud services. Requires legal process (subpoena/warrant) to the provider. Volatile evidence may be lost if not preserved quickly through legal hold requests.

quizzes.topic: Forensic Methodology, Disk, and Memory Forensics

questions.question: You are investigating a suspected data theft. The employee's Windows laptop has been seized. Describe the exact sequence of forensic acquisition steps you would perform, from first touch to having a verified forensic image ready for analysis. Include specific tools and hash verification.
questions.answer: Step 1: Document the scene (photos of laptop state, connections, screen). Step 2: If powered on, acquire volatile memory first using WinPMEM (RAM is lost at power-off). Step 3: Perform a live triage if warranted (running processes, network connections). Step 4: Power down the laptop. Step 5: Remove the hard drive and connect it to the forensic workstation via a hardware write-blocker. Step 6: Create a forensic image using dc3dd (dd with hashing): dc3dd if=/dev/sdX of=evidence.dd hash=sha256 log=acquisition.log. Step 7: Verify the image hash matches the source hash. Step 8: Create a second copy and verify its hash. Step 9: Document everything in the chain of custody form: date, time, examiner, hash values, tools used.

rubric.key: Correct order with memory acquisition first

questions.question: Using Volatility, you run psscan on a memory dump and find a process named 'svchost.exe' with PID 4812 that does NOT appear in the pslist output. What does this discrepancy indicate, and what three Volatility plugins would you run next to investigate?
questions.answer: This indicates the process has been unlinked from the active process list (EPROCESS doubly-linked list), a technique used by rootkits and advanced malware to hide from task managers and pslist. Three investigative plugins: (1) malfind -p 4812: scan the process memory for injected code (executable, non-file-backed memory regions). (2) dlllist -p 4812: list loaded DLLs to identify suspicious libraries or anomalous load paths. (3) netscan or connections filtered to PID 4812: identify any network connections to C2 servers. Additional: cmdline -p 4812 to see the command-line arguments and handles -p 4812 to see open handles to files, registry keys, and mutexes.

questions.question: You are analyzing Windows event logs from a compromised server. You see Event ID 4624 (Logon Type 3) from IP 10.0.1.50, followed by Event ID 4672 (Special Privileges Assigned), then Event ID 7045 (New Service Installed) with a service named 'WindowsUpdate' running 'C:\temp\payload.exe'. Reconstruct the attack sequence and explain the significance of each event.
questions.answer: Attack sequence: (1) Event 4624 Type 3 indicates a network logon from 10.0.1.50, likely using stolen credentials or pass-the-hash. (2) Event 4672 shows the account was granted administrative privileges (SeDebugPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege, etc.), confirming the compromised account has admin rights. (3) Event 7045 shows the attacker installed a service as a persistence mechanism. The suspicious indicators: service name mimics a legitimate Windows service, the binary path is in C:\temp\ (non-standard location), and the sequence happened in rapid succession. The investigator should correlate: check 10.0.1.50 for compromise (it is likely an already-compromised lateral movement source), hash payload.exe for threat intel lookup, and check for similar service installations on other hosts.

rubric.key: Correct interpretation of each event ID
Identifies suspicious indicators and next steps

questions.question: During network forensic analysis of a packet capture, you discover a series of DNS TXT queries from an internal host to 'data.exfil-c2.com' where each query contains a base64-encoded subdomain. The responses contain base64-encoded TXT records. Explain the forensic significance and how you would extract and decode the exfiltrated data.
questions.answer: This is DNS tunneling used for bidirectional C2 communication and data exfiltration. The base64 subdomains encode data being sent from the compromised host to the attacker's authoritative DNS server. The TXT responses encode commands from the attacker back to the malware. Extraction steps: (1) Use tshark to filter DNS queries to exfil-c2.com: tshark -r capture.pcap -Y 'dns.qry.name contains exfil-c2.com' -T fields -e dns.qry.name. (2) Extract the subdomain portions (everything before '.exfil-c2.com'). (3) Base64 decode each subdomain to recover the original exfiltrated data. (4) Similarly extract TXT record responses and base64 decode them to see attacker commands. (5) Reconstruct the complete communication timeline to understand what data was stolen and what commands were executed.

rubric.key: Identifies DNS tunneling for C2 and exfiltration

major_assignments.title: Digital Forensic Investigation
major_assignments.description: Conduct a complete digital forensic investigation of a provided evidence set including a disk image, memory dump, and packet capture. Produce a court-ready forensic report.
objectives: Perform forensic analysis across disk, memory, and network evidence
Construct a comprehensive timeline of events
Identify the attack vector, actions taken, and data compromised
Produce a professional forensic report suitable for legal proceedings
deliverables: Forensic report following standard template (15-20 pages)
Evidence timeline with all sources correlated
Hash verification log for all evidence handling
Executive summary with key findings for non-technical stakeholders

criteria: Proper hash verification of all evidence
Chain of custody documentation maintained
Write-blocking procedures followed
Evidence integrity preserved throughout
All key artifacts identified from disk, memory, and network evidence
Attack vector and entry point determined
Lateral movement and persistence identified
Data exfiltration scope documented
Comprehensive timeline with all evidence sources
Timestamps correlated across different time zones
Key events clearly marked and explained
Gaps in timeline identified and noted
Professional format suitable for legal proceedings
Clear separation of findings and opinions
All conclusions supported by evidence citations
Executive summary accessible to non-technical readers
Thorough forensic analysis across all evidence types
Complete timeline reconstruction with correlated sources
Professional court-ready report with proper evidence citations
Clear and defensible conclusions supported by evidence

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of forensic concepts, methodology, and tool knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on forensic analysis labs with disk, memory, and network evidence

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive forensic investigation with court-ready report

midterm_examination.description: Practical exam: timed forensic analysis of provided evidence

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in course failure.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: All forensic analysis and reports must be individual work. Discussion of methodology is permitted; sharing findings is not.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Evidence must be analyzed as provided. Fabricating or modifying forensic evidence is academic fraud and grounds for expulsion.
course_policies.lab_safety: All forensic tools must be used only on provided evidence within the lab environment. Unauthorized analysis of live systems or personal devices of others is prohibited.
